sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: In Kotlin, how we handle data is centered around two main concepts: immutability (consistency) and type inference (conciseness).

sections.heading: Defining Variables: val vs var
sections.content: Kotlin uses two keywords to declare variables. Choosing the right one is the first step toward writing safe code.

sections.heading: val (Value)
items: Immutable: Once assigned, the value cannot be changed.
Rule of thumb: Always use val by default.

sections.heading: var (Variable)
items: Mutable: The value can be reassigned later.
Use when the data is expected to change (e.g., counter or user input).

sections.heading: val vs var Example
sections.code:
val birthYear = 1995 // Fixed
var currentWeight = 70 // Can change

// birthYear = 1996  <-- Compiler Error
currentWeight = 68   // Perfectly fine

sections.heading: Type Inference
sections.content: Kotlin is statically typed, but you don't always have to write the type explicitly. The compiler infers the type based on the assigned value.

sections.heading: Type Inference Example
sections.code:
val message = "Hello" // String
val age = 25          // Int

sections.heading: Explicit Type Declaration
sections.content: If you want to be explicit or don't assign a value immediately, use the syntax: val variableName: DataType = value

rows.types: Byte, Short, Int, Long, Float, Double

sections.heading: Numerical Literals
items: Float: Suffixed with f or F (e.g., 2.5f)

sections.heading: String Templates
sections.content: Kotlin simplifies string concatenation using the $ symbol. No need to use + to join strings and variables.

sections.heading: String Template Example
sections.code:
val name = "Android"
val version = 14

println("Welcome to $name version $version")
println("The name has ${name.length} characters")

sections.heading: Type Conversion
sections.content: In Kotlin, smaller types like Int are not implicitly converted to larger types like Long. Explicit conversion is required.

sections.heading: Type Conversion Example
Summary
items: Use val for constants and var for variables.
Kotlin usually infers the data type automatically.
String templates ($) are preferred over string concatenation.
Explicit conversion is required between different number types.
